explicit_text: I joined the Seabees in December 1944, specifically the 81st and 94th units. We were tasked with building chow halls and Army rest camps, first at Normandy's Sugar Red Beach and later on Okinawa. Our outfit was mostly older men experienced in construction, but I was one of the younger ones. The Marines initially trained us and armed us before the Navy took over the Seabees officially. I learned a lot from the experience, including how to improvise, like making wash machines and showers from 55-gallon drums.

explicit_text: We were sent to Normandy during World War II, landing at Sugar Red Beach. Our job was to build a chow hall and an Army rest camp in a cow pasture near the shore. We stayed there for several months, building Quonset huts and setting up field kitchens. It was a significant part of my service before we were sent back to the States and then to the South Pacific.

Initially, I was in the Navy, but then I was transferred to the Seabees, which later officially became part of the Navy. We trained at Great Lakes Naval Training Station and served on ships like the USS Hornet. The Navy was my branch throughout the war, and I learned a lot about discipline and service there.

Eventually, the Navy took over the Seabees, but the Marines played a crucial role in our early combat readiness.
